skill.name: scanning-tools
skill.description: Educational guide for security scanning tools including network discovery, vulnerability assessment, web application testing, and compliance validation using industry-standard tools.
skill.summary: Comprehensive documentation for authorized security testing using Nmap, Nessus, Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, and other professional scanning tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 184 patterns without semantic context. All findings are false positives - this is educational documentation containing bash command examples for authorized security testing. The skill explicitly requires written authorization and follows responsible disclosure practices. Risk factors are inherent to security tool documentation, not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Security Tool Documentation Contains Sensitive Patterns
medium_findings.description: Documentation includes examples of security tools (Metasploit, Aircrack-ng) that could be misused without proper authorization. However, the skill explicitly states legal requirements and responsible use guidelines.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Educational content about penetration testing tools is legitimate but requires user awareness of legal boundaries. Skill includes appropriate warnings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Addresses in Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation uses private IP ranges (192.168.1.x) as examples. These are standard documentation practices for network security tutorials, not actual target specifications.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Private IP ranges are standard for educational examples. No evidence of actual targeting of specific systems.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Localhost URLs and example domains (target.com) are used for demonstration purposes. These are placeholder values for educational content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Localhost and placeholder domains are standard documentation practice. No evidence of malicious external endpoints.

low_findings.title: Sudo Commands for Tool Installation
low_findings.description: Documentation shows sudo commands for installing and running security tools (nessusd, openvas, lynis). These are legitimate system administration commands for authorized security testing setups.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sudo commands are necessary for installing security tools on Linux systems. No evidence of privilege escalation for malicious purposes.

content.user_title: Master Security Scanning Tools for Authorized Testing
content.value_statement: Security professionals need comprehensive guidance on using scanning tools effectively and legally. This skill provides structured documentation for network discovery, vulnerability assessment, and compliance validation using industry-standard tools.
actual_capabilities: Execute network discovery scans using Nmap and Masscan with appropriate techniques
Configure and run vulnerability scanners including Nessus and OpenVAS
Perform web application security testing with Burp Suite and OWASP ZAP
Conduct wireless network security assessments using Aircrack-ng suite
Generate compliance reports for CIS, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA frameworks
Produce professional security reports in multiple output formats
limitations: Requires proper authorization before scanning any systems not owned by the user
Cannot execute actual scanning tools - provides documentation and guidance only
Does not replace certified penetration testing tools or professional security assessments
Some advanced features require commercial licenses (Nessus Professional, Burp Suite Pro)

use_cases.title: Internal Security Assessment
use_cases.description: Security teams performing authorized internal network assessments can use this skill to plan and execute comprehensive vulnerability scans with proper documentation.

use_cases.title: Compliance Validation
use_cases.description: IT auditors and compliance officers can leverage scanning tools to validate security controls against regulatory requirements like PCI-DSS and HIPAA.

use_cases.title: Web Application Testing
use_cases.description: Development teams can integrate security scanning into their CI/CD pipelines to identify vulnerabilities before production deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Network Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan the internal network 192.168.1.0/24 for live hosts and open ports. Use appropriate timing to avoid triggering alerts.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial network reconnaissance for authorized internal assessment

prompt_templates.title: Web Application Security Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a comprehensive security scan of https://example-app.com using OWASP ZAP. Focus on OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities and generate an HTML report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pre-deployment security validation for web applications

prompt_templates.title: Vulnerability Assessment Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Run Nessus vulnerability scan against the target subnet and generate a PDF report with executive summary, risk distribution, and remediation recommendations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quarterly vulnerability assessment for compliance requirements

prompt_templates.title: Wireless Security Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Document the process for auditing wireless network security including monitor mode setup, network discovery, and security protocol validation.

output_examples.output: Discovery report showing 15 live hosts, 47 open ports across 8 services. Critical findings: 2 hosts running outdated SMB versions, 1 host with exposed RDP. Recommendations provided for each vulnerability.

Security report identifying 3 medium-severity issues: missing security headers, cookie without secure flag, and verbose error messages. No critical vulnerabilities detected. Remediation steps included.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before scanning any systems you do not own
Start with passive reconnaissance before active scanning to minimize impact
Document all scanning activities and maintain audit trails for compliance
anti_patterns: Scanning systems without explicit written permission - this is illegal in most jurisdictions
Using aggressive scan timing on production systems - can cause service disruption
Ignoring false positive validation - always manually verify critical findings before reporting

faq.question: Do I need special permission to use these scanning tools?
faq.answer: Yes, you must have explicit written authorization from the system owner before scanning. Unauthorized scanning may violate computer crime laws.

faq.question: Can this skill actually execute security scans?
faq.answer: This skill provides documentation and guidance. You must have the actual tools installed (Nmap, Nessus, etc.) and execute commands in your terminal.

faq.question: What is the difference between passive and active scanning?
faq.answer: Passive scanning observes network traffic without sending probes. Active scanning sends packets to targets and may trigger security alerts.

faq.question: How do I avoid false positives in vulnerability scans?
faq.answer: Use credentialed scanning when possible, manually verify critical findings, and correlate results from multiple tools before reporting.

faq.question: Which scanning tool is best for web applications?
faq.answer: Burp Suite Professional and OWASP ZAP are industry standards. Burp offers more advanced features; ZAP is free and open-source.

faq.question: How often should I run vulnerability scans?
faq.answer: For compliance (PCI-DSS), quarterly scans are required. Best practice is continuous scanning with each significant change to infrastructure.
